policy_statement: Implement Complete Streets and Vision Zero pedestrian and cyclist safety programs.
mechanism_description: Redesigning streets and implementing traffic-safety programs makes walking and cycling safer and more attractive, shifting trips away from cars and reducing transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Street design + safety programs → mode shift/↓ VMT + fewer car trips → ↓ fuel combustion in vehicles → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Urban street right-of-way design and operations are reconfigured (e.g., protected bike lanes, safer crossings, traffic calming, lower speeds, better sidewalks) and paired with safety programs/enforcement/education, improving perceived and actual safety for active travel.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased safety and network connectivity reduces the generalized “cost” (risk, time, stress) of walking/cycling, inducing mode shift and/or shorter car trips; reduced VMT lowers gasoline/diesel combustion (and can reduce congestion/idling in some corridors).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower combustion of transport fuels leads to reduced CO₂ (and co-pollutant) emissions from the transport sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road transport GHG emissions due to mode shift from private car trips to walking and cycling (and associated VMT reduction).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Built-environment and safety interventions → increased active travel attractiveness/safety → mode shift and reduced VMT → reduced fuel combustion → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary climate effect is direct reduction of emissions from transportation energy use; resource-efficiency framing is secondary because the key outcome is ↓ GHG from avoided car travel rather than primarily “same service with less energy” inside the same mode.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some Complete Streets elements (trees, shade) can have adaptation or nature-based co-benefits, and safety programs can be implemented without sizable mode shift; however, for this canonical mechanism as defined (“modal shift and transport emissions reduction”), the dominant pathway is mitigation via avoided vehicle emissions.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is Complete Streets/Vision Zero → safer walking/cycling → mode shift away from cars will primarily reduce transport emissions, fitting Mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy also substantively advances adaptation (heat risk reduction), nature-based solutions (street trees), or broader resource efficiency depends on policy-specific design and evidence and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented mainly as a safety/enforcement program with minimal infrastructure change (or in contexts with low latent demand for walking/cycling), emissions reductions may be modest; conversely, adding substantial street trees/green infrastructure could introduce a meaningful Nature-Based or Adaptation secondary pathway, but the primary mechanism here remains transport mitigation via mode shift.
key_indicators: "Implement"; "Complete Streets"; "Vision Zero"; "pedestrian and cyclist safety programs"
co_benefits: Road safety/public health benefits (reduced traffic injuries/fatalities, increased physical activity); potential extreme-heat comfort benefits if paired with shade/streetscape measures (not specified).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via regulatory mandates (Complete Streets ordinance) and/or infrastructure investment (bike lanes/sidewalks), but the text frames it as implementing programs/frameworks without specifying an ordinance, standard, or capital spend—so classified as planning/strategy.
